reasoning: In Serbia, the resignation of the Prime Minister is often used by President Aleksandar Vučić as a constitutional mechanism to trigger snap parliamentary elections, rather than to merely form a new government. Vučić has employed this tactic repeatedly (most recently in late 2023 with Ana Brnabić's resignation) to secure new mandates during periods of public protest or political tension. Under the Serbian Constitution, once the PM's resignation is confirmed by the National Assembly, there is a 30-day window to elect a new government. If no new government is formed, the President must dissolve parliament and call elections. Assuming the resignation was confirmed near the May 15, 2025 knowledge cutoff, this 30-day window would expire in June, culminating in the announcement of an election date well before July 1, 2025. While there's a small chance the ruling SNS party simply appoints a new PM to avoid elections (hence a probability less than 1), Vučić's established playbook points heavily towards an election announcement.
